channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Gala Shooting Suspect Was Targeting Trump Administration Officials, Writings Reveal
item.description: A man being held in connection with the attack wrote a note indicating potential targets and grievances, authorities say.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin and Chelsia Rose Marcius
item.media:credit: Damian Dovarganes/Associated Press
item.media:description: A cameraman stands in front of a California home connected to Cole Tomas Allen, who is being held in connection to an attack at a gala in Washington, D.C., according to officials.

item.title: ‘Shots Fired!’: Inside the Pandemonium at the Washington Hilton
item.description: Guests dived to the floor and took cover as Secret Service agents climbed over tables to protect some of the country’s most high-ranking officials, including President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater, Shawn McCreesh, Tyler Pager and Maggie Haberman
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times

item.title: After Correspondents Dinner Shooting, Rumors and Conspiracy Theories Swirl Online
item.description: Influencers jumped to fill the information void with conspiracy theories about the attack at the White House Correspondents’ dinner on Saturday.
item.dc:creator: Stuart A. Thompson, Tiffany Hsu and Steven Lee Myers
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Security outside the Washington Hilton after gun shots were fired during the 2026 White House Correspondents’ Association Dinner Saturday night.

item.title: Security at Correspondents’ Dinner Worked as Intended, Experts Say
item.description: Officials noted that the suspect never made it into the hotel ballroom, where President Trump and hundreds of journalists were gathered for the White House correspondents’ dinner.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett, Eileen Sullivan and Chelsia Rose Marcius
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: The scene in the hallways after shots were fired during the 2026 White House Correspondents’ Association Dinner at the Washington Hilton on Saturday.

item.title: Thom Tillis Is Prepared to Advance Kevin Warsh After U.S. Drops Fed Inquiry
item.description: Senator Thom Tillis said he had received assurances from federal prosecutors that eased his concerns, setting the stage for a key committee vote on Kevin Warsh.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Thom Tillis in the Capitol this month.

item.title: The Hard Life of an Immigrant Whose Killing Became a Symbol for Trump
item.description: President Trump posted surveillance footage of Nilufa Easmin’s brutal killing by another immigrant to advance his agenda. Behind the rhetoric was a more nuanced story.
item.dc:creator: David Ovalle and Patricia Mazzei

item.title: U.S. Military Strikes Another Boat in Eastern Pacific, Killing 3
item.description: The United States has carried out dozens of boat strikes since September against people the Trump administration accuses of smuggling drugs.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:credit: US Southern Command
item.media:description: An image from a video provided by the U.S. military shows what it said was a strike on a boat in the eastern Pacific Ocean on Sunday.

item.title: Family Detained for Months at Texas ICE Facility Released
item.description: Two days after their release, the mother and her five children were again detained during an ICE check-in. A judge halted their removal and returned the family home.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Eric Gay/Associated Press
item.media:description: Hayam El Gamal and her five children spent nearly a year in an immigration detention facility in Dilley, Texas.

item.title: Iran and U.S. Sink Into Awkward Limbo of ‘No War, No Peace’
item.description: Each side is betting it can last longer than the other, analysts say. But there are risks in a stalemate without a deal.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural in Tehran depicting Iranian missiles attacking a U.S. Navy ship.

item.title: What to Know About U.S.-Iran Peace Talks
item.description: An Iranian negotiator returned to Pakistan on Sunday, despite the United States abruptly calling off a trip there by its two top negotiators.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Aamir Qureshi/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Posters highlighting talks in Islamabad, Pakistan, on Saturday.

item.title: Muted Reaction as Oil and Stock Markets Reopen
item.description: Oil prices rose and stocks fell after President Trump called off a trip to Pakistan by two U.S. negotiators for peace talks with Iran.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Spencer Platt/Getty Images

item.title: Can King Charles Help Heal the U.S.-British Rupture?
item.description: Not since his mother, Queen Elizabeth II, traveled to Washington after the Suez Crisis has a visit by the British monarch come at such a fraught time in Anglo-American relations.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
Elizabeth II, Queen of Great Britain
item.media:credit: Andrew Caballero-Reynolds/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: King Charles III, center, and President Trump with a Coldstream Guardsman in September at Windsor Castle.

item.title: 8 Memorable Moments From Past British Royal Visits to the U.S.
item.description: With King Charles III and Queen Camilla set to arrive for a state visit with President Trump, we look back at some major moments in previous royal trips to the United States.
item.dc:creator: Esther Bintliff and Sarah Eckinger
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
Elizabeth II, Queen of Great Britain
George VI, King of Great Britain
item.media:credit: Eddie Sanderson/Getty Images
item.media:description: Queen Elizabeth and President Ronald Reagan in San Francisco, in 1983.

item.title: The Mother Who Will Not Speak
item.description: When Jacqueline Pritchett’s 11-year-old son, Jacob, vanished last year, she refused to acknowledge that he existed. Her life is as mysterious as his disappearance.
item.dc:creator: Maria Cramer
item.media:credit: Dakota Santiago for The New York Times
item.media:description: The disappearance of 11-year-old Jacob Pritchett has baffled police officers who are frustrated that his mother, Jacqueline Pritchett, refuses to acknowledge that he ever existed.

item.title: The Rising Chinese Automaker Not Named BYD
item.description: Geely is challenging the giant BYD by adapting quickly to swings in demand and energy prices, seizing on interest in electric vehicles prompted by the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times

item.title: Former Israeli Premiers Join in Bid to Oust Netanyahu in Elections
item.description: Naftali Bennett, a right-wing politician, and Yair Lapid, a centrist, will merge parties for a vote later this year.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner
item.media:credit: Ariel Schalit/Associated Press
item.media:description: Naftali Bennett, left, and Yair Lapid on Sunday in Herzliya, Israel, where they said they would unite in a party to be called Yachad, Hebrew for “together.”

item.title: An Unthinkable Extreme of Domestic Violence: Killing Multiple Relatives
item.description: The Shreveport, La., slaying of eight children was an example of what experts call family annihilation.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images
item.media:description: A memorial set up in Shreveport, La., after a father killed eight children, seven of whom were his, according to the police.

item.title: With Homicides and Other Violent Crimes at Record Lows, Funding for Prevention Falls
item.description: Homicides and assaults have declined in many cities, but programs credited with helping keep the peace are losing federal support.
item.dc:creator: Shayla Colon
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: A group of boys play basketball with Cure Violence staff members in Annapolis, Md.

item.title: California Billionaire Tax Has Enough Signatures to Land on Ballot, Backers Say
item.description: The measure calls for placing a one-time 5 percent tax on the assets of California residents with at least $1.1 billion. Opponents are backing competing measures to counter the tax.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:credit: Mike Kai Chen for The New York Times
item.media:description: The billionaire tax’s supporters, who have been gathering signatures for months, promoted the measure at a booth at the California Democratic Convention in November.

item.title: New Taxes Helped Cool London’s Housing Market. Could That Happen in New York?
item.description: Economists and real estate agents are calling London’s taxation of wealthy property owners a cautionary tale for New York, where leaders have endorsed a second-home tax.
item.dc:creator: Debra Kamin
item.media:credit: Sam Bush for The New York Times

item.title: Chevy Humphrey Is Running Experiments at the Griffin Science Museum in Chicago
item.description: Chevy Humphrey explains why the scientific method matters in business.
item.dc:creator: Jordyn Holman
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times

item.title: Rising Costs Are Causing Couples to Delay or Forgo Having Children
item.description: High mortgage payments, higher child care costs and economic uncertainty are making some people rethink their plans on starting a family.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.media:credit: Lindsay D'Addato for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brock Goodwin, left, and Rilee Stewart had long expected that they would have several children. But after weighing all the costs, they decided not to have children at all.

item.title: Targeted Hunts Were Supposed to Curb ‘Zombie Deer Disease.’ Now What?
item.description: In Illinois and other states, officials hoped that culls could halt the progress of chronic wasting disease. Now they are losing hope.
item.dc:creator: Jim Robbins and Julia Rendleman
item.media:credit: Julia Rendleman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Caught on a trail cam on the property of the photographer Julia Rendleman in Makanda, Ill.

item.title: ‘It Wasn’t Real, but It Was Real’
item.description: How ICE transformed a Chicago neighborhood.
item.dc:creator: Megan K. Stack
item.media:credit: Jonathan Michael Castillo for The New York Times

item.title: Is the Supreme Court Coming Apart at the Seams?
item.description: A revealing glimpse of the state of the Supreme Court, on the verge of momentous rulings in the weeks ahead.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Wegman
item.media:credit: Damon Winter/The New York Times

item.title: Powerful Storm Hits North Texas and Kills 2 as Thousands Remain Without Power
item.description: Thousands remain without power as extensive damage occurred across two counties northwest of Fort Worth.
item.dc:creator: Bernard Mokam
item.media:credit: Tony Gutierrez/Associated Press
item.media:description: At least 11,000 people remain without power, according to Jason Lane, the emergency management coordinator for Parker County.

item.title: The Apocalypse Goes Mainstream
item.description: About 40 percent of American adults believe that we are living in the “end times,” according to a 2022 poll. Where did that idea come from?
item.dc:creator: Lauren Jackson
item.media:credit: Mason Marsh/Associated Press
item.media:description: The Ruby Ridge standoff in Idaho, in August 1992.

item.title: South Carolina Measles Outbreak Ends After Sickening Nearly 1,000
item.description: It was the largest outbreak in recent U.S. history.
item.dc:creator: Teddy Rosenbluth
item.media:credit: Sean Rayford/Getty Images
item.media:description: A mobile clinic offered free measles, mumps and rubella vaccinations amid a measles outbreak in South Carolina.
